denials.cite: without conceding that the word is assembled from that pattern: fit, not composition

claims.cite: As the fitness between an apprehension and its object is established and fixed

claims.name: vyanjya-vyanjaka

rejected_readings.label: compositional_assembly
rejected_readings.rendering: The word is assembled or composed from the right combination of phonemes according to a structural principle.
rejected_readings.why: The commentary explicitly rejects this: 'without conceding that the word is assembled from that pattern: fit, not composition.' The point is that yogyatā (fitness) explains the manifestation of sphota by sounds without making the word composed of sounds. Asserting asraya (grounding-in) from sounds to sphota contradicts this denial.

rejected_readings.label: derived_fitness
rejected_readings.rendering: The fitness between sound and word-meaning is derived from or explained by causal processes or learned associations.
rejected_readings.why: The verse insists that yogyatā is siddha (established) and niyata (fixed)—bedrock properties that cannot be derived or explained further. To derive fitness from causes would contradict these essential predicates and violate the 'primitive' status the commentary assigns to this relation.

notes: Verse 1.97 establishes yogyatā (fitness/aptitude) as a primitive, bedrock relation in both cognition and language. Just as the fitness between perception and its object is unexplained yet given (siddha, niyata), so too is the fitness between sphota and sounds, in their vyañjya-vyañjaka (manifested-manifestor) mode. The contract captures this through dual yogyate relations and shared predicates on yogyatā. One denial explicitly rules out asraya (grounding) from sounds to sphota, blocking the reductive 'assembly' interpretation. The framework preserves the verse's ontological commitments without strain.
